steps.title: Focus on the caution
steps.content: The researchers found that zoo chimpanzees in mid-latitude countries had lower vitamin D levels in winter than in summer. They also observed no clear effect from vitamin D supplements, but the supplement records were rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked. Therefore, their caution concerns what cannot be concluded from weak supplement data.

steps.title: Reject unrelated conclusions
steps.content: Choice A compares seasonal patterns in zoo and wild chimpanzees, not whether supplements work. Choice B says the lack of an observed supplement effect may reflect data shortcomings; that is the researchers' caution itself, not the conclusion they warn against. Choice D follows from the UVB and seasonal vitamin D finding, not from the flawed supplement records.

steps.title: Avoid overreading supplements
steps.content: The unsupported leap is to say supplementation has no promise as a strategy for offsetting winter reductions in vitamin D levels. Since the supplementation data were too inconsistent to support that broad negative conclusion, the researchers would caution against it. The answer is C.
